lem:iterator-relation-consistency.title: Consistency of the Minimal Iterator Relation
lem:iterator-relation-consistency.body:
Let `(P,S,1)` be a Peano system, let `(W,c,g)` be iterator data for it, and
let `R^*` be the minimal iterator relation for `(W,c,g)`. Then `R^*` is an
iterator relation for `(W,c,g)`.

Define

```text
I = { R subset P x W : R is an iterator relation for (W,c,g) }.
```

Define

```text
R^* = intersection over R in I of R.
```

Since every `R` in `I` is an iterator relation, each contains `(1,c)`, so
`(1,c)` belongs to `R^*`.

Let `(n,w)` be arbitrary in `R^*`. Since `(n,w)` belongs to every `R` in `I`,
and every `R` in `I` is an iterator relation, `(S(n),g(w))` belongs to every
`R` in `I`. Hence `(S(n),g(w))` belongs to `R^*`.

Therefore the minimal iterator relation is an iterator relation for `(W,c,g)`.

thm:no-object-is-its-own-successor.title: No Object Is Its Own Successor
thm:no-object-is-its-own-successor.body:
Let `(P,S,1)` be a Peano system. For every `n in P`, `S(n) != n`.

Define

```text
D = { x in P : S(x) != x }.
```

Proceed by induction.

Base case: `1 in D`, because `1 in P` and `S(1) != 1`; otherwise `1` would
be a successor, contrary to the Peano axiom that the distinguished element is
not a successor.

Inductive step: assume `S(n) != n`. By the contrapositive of successor
injectivity, equality `S(S(n)) = S(n)` would imply `S(n) = n`, so
`S(S(n)) != S(n)`. Hence `S(n) in D`.

Therefore `D` contains `1` and is closed under successor. By induction,
`D = P`. Hence for every `n in P`, `S(n) != n`.

prop:bounds-sum-set.title: Bounds for Suprema and Infima Under Set Addition
prop:bounds-sum-set.body:
Let

```text
A + B := {a + b : a in A, b in B}.
```

Let `u` be an upper bound of `A + B`. Let

```text
s_a = sup A
s_b = sup B.
```

For every `a in A` and `b in B`, `a <= s_a` and `b <= s_b`; therefore

```text
a + b <= s_a + s_b.
```

Hence `s_a + s_b` is an upper bound of `A + B`. Therefore

```text
sup(A + B) <= sup A + sup B.
```

prop:maximum-implies-supremum.title: Maximum Implies Supremum
prop:maximum-implies-supremum.body:
Assume `A` is nonempty and bounded above. Suppose

```text
b in A
```

and `b` is an upper bound of `A`.

Let `B` be the set of upper bounds of `A`. For every `a in A`, `a <= b`.

Let `b' in B` be arbitrary. Since `b'` is an upper bound of `A` and `b in A`,
we have

```text
b <= b'.
```

Thus `b` is an upper bound of `A`, and `b` is less than or equal to every
upper bound of `A`. Therefore `b` is the least upper bound of `A`, so `b` is
the supremum of `A`.

prop:uniqueness-of-the-supremum.title: Uniqueness of the Supremum
prop:uniqueness-of-the-supremum.body:
Assume `s_0` is the supremum of `S`, and assume `s_1` is another supremum of
`S`.

Then `s_0` and `s_1` are both upper bounds of `S`.

Since `s_0` is the least upper bound and `s_1` is an upper bound, `s_0 <= s_1`.

Since `s_1` is the least upper bound and `s_0` is an upper bound, `s_1 <= s_0`.

Therefore

```text
s_0 <= s_1 <= s_0.
```

Hence `s_0 = s_1`.

thm:scalar-mult-positive.title: Scalar Multiplication by a Positive Scalar
thm:scalar-mult-positive.body:
Let `c > 0` be constant. Let

```text
cA = {ca : a in A}.
```

Let `s_a = sup A`. For every `a in A`,

```text
a <= s_a.
```

Multiplying by `c > 0` gives

```text
ca <= c s_a.
```

Therefore `c s_a` is an upper bound of `cA`.

Let `epsilon > 0` be arbitrary. By the epsilon characterization of the
supremum, there exists `a in A` such that

```text
s_a - epsilon/c < a.
```

Multiplying by `c` gives

```text
c s_a - epsilon < ca.
```

Since `ca in cA`, every number below `c s_a` fails to be an upper bound of
`cA`. Therefore `c s_a` is the least upper bound of `cA`.

Hence

```text
sup(cA) = c sup A.
```

thm:supremum-is-monotone-under-inclusion.title: Supremum Is Monotone Under Inclusion
thm:supremum-is-monotone-under-inclusion.body:
Assume `A subset B` and both sets are bounded.

Let

```text
s_b = sup B.
```

Since `s_b` is an upper bound of `B`,

```text
b <= s_b    for every b in B.
```

Because `A subset B`, every `a in A` is also in `B`. Hence

```text
a <= s_b    for every a in A.
```

Thus `s_b` is an upper bound of `A`. Since `s_a = sup A` is the least upper
bound of `A`, we get

```text
s_a <= s_b.
```

Therefore `sup A <= sup B`.

thm:supremum-sum-set.title: Supremum of a Sum Set
thm:supremum-sum-set.body:
Let `A` and `B` be nonempty subsets of `R` that are bounded above. Define

```text
A + B = {a + b : a in A, b in B}.
```

Let

```text
s_a = sup A
s_b = sup B
s_ab = sup(A + B).
```

For every `a in A` and `b in B`,

```text
a <= s_a
b <= s_b.
```

Adding gives

```text
a + b <= s_a + s_b.
```

Therefore `s_a + s_b` is an upper bound of `A + B`, so

```text
s_ab <= s_a + s_b.
```

Let `epsilon > 0` be arbitrary. There exist `a in A` and `b in B` such that

```text
s_a - epsilon/2 < a
s_b - epsilon/2 < b.
```

Adding gives

```text
s_a + s_b - epsilon < a + b <= s_ab.
```

Hence `s_a + s_b <= s_ab`. Combining both inequalities,

```text
s_ab = s_a + s_b.
```

Therefore `sup(A+B)=sup A+sup B`.

thm:translation-invariance-supremum.title: Translation Invariance of the Supremum
thm:translation-invariance-supremum.body:
Let `c in R`. Prove that

```text
sup(A + c) = sup A + c.
```

Let `s_a = sup A`. Then for every `a in A`,

```text
a <= s_a.
```

Adding `c` gives

```text
a + c <= s_a + c.
```

Thus `s_a + c` is an upper bound of `A + c`.

Let `s_c = sup(A + c)`. Since `s_a + c` is an upper bound of `A + c`,

```text
s_c <= s_a + c.
```

Conversely, for every `a in A`, `a + c <= s_c`. Subtracting `c` gives

```text
a <= s_c - c.
```

Thus `s_c - c` is an upper bound of `A`. Since `s_a = sup A`,

```text
s_a <= s_c - c.
```

Therefore

```text
s_a + c <= s_c.
```

Combining the two inequalities gives `s_c = s_a + c`, so
`sup(A + c) = sup A + c`.

prop:convergent-bounded.title: Convergent Sequences Are Bounded
prop:convergent-bounded.body:
Let `(a_n)` be a sequence and suppose `a_n -> L`.

By convergence, for every `epsilon > 0` there exists `N in N` such that for
all `n >= N`, `|a_n - L| < epsilon`.

Choose `epsilon = 1`. Then there exists `N in N` such that for all `n >= N`,
`|a_n - L| < 1`.

For `n >= N`,

```text
|a_n| = |(a_n - L) + L|
      <= |a_n - L| + |L|
      < 1 + |L|.
```

Thus the tail is bounded by `1 + |L|`.

For the finitely many initial terms, define

```text
M_0 = max{|a_1|, |a_2|, ..., |a_{N-1}|}
```

if `N > 1`; if `N = 1`, take `M_0 = 0`.

Choose

```text
M = max{M_0, 1 + |L|}.
```

Then `M > 0`, and for every `n in N`, `|a_n| <= M`. If `n < N`, this follows
from the definition of `M_0`; if `n >= N`, this follows from the tail bound.
Therefore `(a_n)` is bounded.

prop:convergent-bounded.title: Convergent Sequences Are Bounded: Contrapositive Proof
prop:convergent-bounded.body:
Assume `(a_n)` is unbounded. By unboundedness,

```text
for every M > 0, there exists n such that |a_n| > M.
```

Let `L in R` be arbitrary. Choose `M = |L| + 1`. Then there exists `n` such
that

```text
|a_n| > |L| + 1.
```

By the reverse triangle inequality,

```text
|a_n - L| >= ||a_n| - |L||.
```

The chosen `n` gives `|a_n| - |L| > 1`, so `|a_n - L| > 1`.

Now let `epsilon_0 = 1`. For every threshold `N`, unboundedness gives some
`n >= N` with `|a_n| > |L| + 1`, hence `|a_n - L| > epsilon_0`.

Thus for every `L in R`, convergence to `L` fails. Since `L` was arbitrary,
the sequence does not converge.

prop:eps-char-sup.title: Epsilon Characterisation of Supremum
prop:eps-char-sup.body:
Show that if `s = sup A`, then for every `epsilon > 0` there exists `a in A`
with

```text
s - epsilon < a <= s.
```

Assume `s = sup A`.

By the definition of supremum, `s` is an upper bound for `A`.

Hence

```text
a <= s    for all a in A.
```

Let `epsilon > 0` be arbitrary. Since `s` is the least upper bound of `A`,
`s - epsilon` is not an upper bound of `A`.

Therefore there exists `a in A` such that

```text
s - epsilon < a.
```

Since `s` is an upper bound, `a <= s`. Hence

```text
s - epsilon < a <= s.
```

Thus the claim holds.

thm:bolzano-weierstrass-sequences.title: Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem for Sequences
thm:bolzano-weierstrass-sequences.body:
# Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem for Sequences

Source: one handwritten proof photo preserved as a sanitized image.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:bolzano-weierstrass-sequences`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/subsequences/notes-subsequences.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof combines the monotone subsequence theorem with boundedness. A
bounded real sequence has a monotone subsequence, and that subsequence remains
bounded. The monotone convergence theorem then gives convergence of the
selected subsequence.

## Attachment Note

thm:boundedness-passes-to-subsequences.title: Boundedness Passes to Subsequences
thm:boundedness-passes-to-subsequences.body:
# Boundedness Passes to Subsequences

Source: one handwritten proof photo preserved as a sanitized image.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:boundedness-passes-to-subsequences`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/subsequences/notes-subsequences.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof uses the same uniform bound for the original sequence and its
subsequence. If `|x_n| <= M` for all `n`, then every subsequence term
`x_{n_k}` is one of the original sequence terms, so `|x_{n_k}| <= M` for all
`k`. Hence the subsequence is bounded.

## Attachment Note

The canonical theorem has a statement label, but no matching canonical proof
file exists yet, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:cauchy-criterion-real-sequences.title: Cauchy Criterion for Real Sequences
thm:cauchy-criterion-real-sequences.body:
# Cauchy Criterion for Real Sequences

Source: one handwritten proof photo preserved as a sanitized image.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:cauchy-criterion-real-sequences`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/cauchy/notes-cauchy.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof uses completeness through Bolzano-Weierstrass. A Cauchy sequence is
bounded, so it has a convergent subsequence. The Cauchy condition then keeps
the full tail close to that convergent subsequence, which forces the whole
sequence to converge to the same limit.

## Attachment Note

The canonical theorem has a statement label, but no matching canonical proof
file exists yet, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof fixes the Cauchy tolerance `1`, so all sufficiently late terms are
within `1` of a chosen tail term. Taking the maximum of the finitely many
initial absolute values together with the resulting tail bound gives a single
finite bound for the entire sequence.

thm:constant-comparison-for-sequence-lim.title: Constant Comparison for Sequence Limits
thm:constant-comparison-for-sequence-lim.body:
# Constant Comparison for Sequence Limits

Source: one handwritten proof photo.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:constant-comparison-for-sequence-limits`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/convergence/notes-limits.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof specializes eventual order preservation to a constant comparison: if
`x_n <= b` for all sufficiently large `n` and `x_n -> x`, then the constant
sequence `b_n=b` converges to `b`, so the order-limit theorem gives `x <= b`.

## Attachment Note

The canonical theorem has a proof hyperlink target, but the corresponding
proof file is currently missing, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:convergence-of-tail.title: Convergence of a Tail
thm:convergence-of-tail.body:
# Convergence of a Tail

Source: two handwritten proof photos preserved as separate sanitized images.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:convergence-of-tail`

Canonical proof file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/proofs/notes/prf-convergence-of-tail.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof covers both directions. First, if `x_n -> L`, then for any fixed
tail offset `k`, the tail sequence `y_n = x_{n+k}` also satisfies the same
epsilon threshold after shifting indices. Conversely, if a tail sequence
converges to `L`, then choosing `n` beyond the fixed tail offset and the tail's
epsilon threshold gives `|x_n-L| < epsilon` for all sufficiently large `n`, so
the full sequence converges to the same limit.

## Attachment Note

The canonical proof file exists and currently contains a TODO proof body, so
the proof-vault backlink was attached directly after the Return remark.

thm:convergent-sequences-are-cauchy.title: Convergent Sequences Are Cauchy
thm:convergent-sequences-are-cauchy.body:
Let `(x_n)` be a convergent sequence, and suppose `x_n -> L`.

Let `epsilon > 0`. By convergence, there exists `N in N` such that for all
`n >= N`,

```text
|x_n - L| < epsilon / 2.
```

If `m, n >= N`, then

```text
|x_m - L| < epsilon / 2
|x_n - L| < epsilon / 2.
```

By the triangle inequality,

```text
|x_m - x_n|
  <= |x_m - L| + |x_n - L|
  < epsilon / 2 + epsilon / 2
  = epsilon.
```

Therefore `(x_n)` is Cauchy.

The proof fixes a positive lower bound for `|b_n|` from `b_n -> b != 0`, then

thm:limit-of-a-sum.title: Limit of a Sum
thm:limit-of-a-sum.body:
# Limit of a Sum

Source: two handwritten proof photos preserved as separate sanitized images.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:limit-of-a-sum`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/convergence/notes-algebra.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof uses the standard `epsilon/2` split. If `a_n -> a` and `b_n -> b`,
then for sufficiently large `n`, both `|a_n-a|` and `|b_n-b|` are less than
`epsilon/2`; the triangle inequality then gives convergence of `a_n+b_n` to
`a+b`.

proof file is currently missing, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:limit-of-an-absolute-value.title: Limit of an Absolute Value
thm:limit-of-an-absolute-value.body:
# Limit of an Absolute Value

Source: one handwritten proof photo preserved as a sanitized image.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:limit-of-an-absolute-value`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/convergence/notes-algebra.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof uses the reverse triangle inequality. If `x_n -> x`, then
`||x_n|-|x|| <= |x_n-x|`, so the same eventual epsilon bound proving
convergence of `x_n` to `x` proves convergence of `|x_n|` to `|x|`.

The canonical theorem has a statement label, but no matching canonical proof
file exists yet, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:limit-preserves-eventual-order.title: Limit Preserves Eventual Order
thm:limit-preserves-eventual-order.body:
# Limit Preserves Eventual Order

Source: one handwritten proof photo, with earlier duplicate/supporting views
noted in metadata.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:limit-preserves-eventual-order`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/convergence/notes-limits.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof argues by contradiction. If `x_n <= y_n`, `x_n -> x`, and
`y_n -> y`, but `y < x`, choose `epsilon = (x-y)/2`. For sufficiently large
`n`, `x_n` is within `epsilon` of `x` and `y_n` is within `epsilon` of `y`,
forcing `y_n < x_n`, contradicting the eventual order.

## Attachment Note

The canonical theorem has a proof hyperlink target, but the corresponding
proof file is currently missing, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:monotone-convergence-theorem.title: Monotone Convergence Theorem
thm:monotone-convergence-theorem.body:
Let `(a_n)` be an increasing real sequence bounded above.

Define

```text
A = { a_n : n in N }.
```

Since `(a_n)` is bounded above, `A` is bounded above. By completeness, let

```text
L = sup A.
```

For every `n`, `a_n <= L`.

Let `epsilon > 0`. By the supremum approximation property, there exists
`N in N` such that

```text
a_N > L - epsilon.
```

If `n >= N`, then monotonicity gives

```text
a_N <= a_n <= L.
```

Combining the inequalities,

```text
L - epsilon < a_n <= L.
```

Therefore

```text
|a_n - L| < epsilon
```

for all `n >= N`. Hence `a_n -> L`, and `L = sup {a_n : n in N}`.

thm:monotone-subsequence-theorem.title: Monotone Subsequence Theorem
thm:monotone-subsequence-theorem.body:
# Monotone Subsequence Theorem

Source: one handwritten proof photo preserved as a sanitized image.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:monotone-subsequence-theorem`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/subsequences/notes-subsequences.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof uses the standard peak dichotomy. If the sequence has infinitely
many peaks, selecting peak indices in increasing order gives a decreasing
subsequence. If there are only finitely many peaks, then after the last peak
one can recursively choose later indices with nondecreasing values, producing
an increasing subsequence. Hence every real sequence has a monotone
subsequence.

## Attachment Note

The canonical theorem has a statement label, but no matching canonical proof
file exists yet, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:polynomial-sequence-limit.title: Polynomial Sequence Limit
thm:polynomial-sequence-limit.body:
# Polynomial Sequence Limit

Source: one handwritten proof photo preserved as a sanitized image.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:polynomial-sequence-limit`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/convergence/notes-algebra.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof reduces a polynomial expression to the algebra of limits. Constants
are constant sequences, powers are repeated products, and the polynomial is a
finite sum of scalar multiples of powers. The sum, product, and scalar
multiple laws therefore imply `p(x_n) -> p(L)` whenever `x_n -> L`.

## Attachment Note

The canonical theorem has a statement label, but no matching canonical proof
file exists yet, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:subsequences-preserve-limits.title: Subsequences Preserve Limits
thm:subsequences-preserve-limits.body:
# Subsequences Preserve Limits

Source: one handwritten proof photo preserved as a sanitized image.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:subsequences-preserve-limits`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/subsequences/notes-subsequences.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof argues directly from the definition of convergence. Given
`a_n -> L` and a subsequence `a_{n_k}`, choose `N` so that `|a_n-L| < epsilon`
for all `n >= N`. Since subsequence indices eventually dominate the identity,
`n_k >= N` for all sufficiently large `k`, and therefore
`|a_{n_k}-L| < epsilon`.

## Attachment Note

The canonical theorem has a statement label, but no matching canonical proof
file exists yet, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:unbounded-monotone-divergence.title: Unbounded Monotone Divergence
thm:unbounded-monotone-divergence.body:
# Unbounded Monotone Divergence

Source: one handwritten proof photo preserved as a sanitized image.

Canonical theorem label: `thm:unbounded-monotone-divergence`

Canonical statement file:
`volume-iii/analysis/sequences/notes/monotonicity/notes-monotonicity.tex`

## Review

Status: `reviewed-correct`

The proof treats the increasing case by using unboundedness above: for every
threshold `M`, some term exceeds `M`, and monotonicity keeps all later terms
above `M`. Hence the sequence diverges to `+infty`. The decreasing case is
the analogous argument for divergence to `-infty`.

## Attachment Note

The canonical theorem has a statement label, but no matching canonical proof
file exists yet, so the proof-vault backlink is deferred.

thm:uniqueness-of-limits.title: Uniqueness of Limits
thm:uniqueness-of-limits.body:
Let `(a_n)` be a convergent sequence with limits `L_1` and `L_2`.
Assume, for contradiction, that `L_1 != L_2`.

Let

```text
epsilon_0 = |L_1 - L_2| / 2 > 0.
```

Since `a_n -> L_1`, there exists `N_1 in N` such that for all `n >= N_1`,

```text
|a_n - L_1| < epsilon_0.
```

Since `a_n -> L_2`, there exists `N_2 in N` such that for all `n >= N_2`,

```text
|a_n - L_2| < epsilon_0.
```

Choose `n >= max(N_1,N_2)`. Then both inequalities hold. By the triangle
inequality,

```text
|L_1 - L_2|
  = |L_1 - a_n + a_n - L_2|
  <= |L_1 - a_n| + |a_n - L_2|
  < epsilon_0 + epsilon_0
  = 2 epsilon_0.
```

But `2 epsilon_0 = |L_1-L_2|`, a contradiction. Therefore `L_1 = L_2`, so the
limit of the sequence is unique.
